question:
For a positive integer $N$, let $f_N$\footnote{Corrected from $F_N$ in the source.} be the function defined by 
\[
f_N(x) = \sum_{n=0}^N \frac{N+1/2-n}{(N+1)(2n+1)} \sin((2n+1)x).
\]
Determine the smallest constant $M$ such that $f_N(x) \leq M$ for all $N$ and all real $x$.
solution:
The smallest constant $M$ is $\pi/4$.

We start from the expression
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq1}
f_N(x) = \sum_{n=0}^N \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{2n+1} - \frac{1}{N+1} \right) \sin((2n+1)x).
\end{equation}
Note that if $\sin(x) > 0$, then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{n=0}^N \sin((2n+1)x) &= \frac{1}{2i} \sum_{n=0}^N (e^{i(2n+1)x} - e^{-i(2n+1)x}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2N+3)x} - e^{ix}}{e^{2ix} - 1}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2N+3)x} - e^{-ix}}{e^{-2ix} - 1} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2N+2)x} - 1}{e^{ix} - e^{-ix}}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2N+2)x} - 1}{e^{-ix} - e^{ix}} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \frac{e^{i(2N+2)x}+ e^{-i(2N+2)x} - 2}{e^{ix} - e^{-ix}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cos ((2N+2)x) - 2}{2i(2i \sin(x))} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos ((2N+2)x)}{2\sin(x)} \geq 0.
\end{align*}
We use this to compare the expressions of $f_N(x)$ and $f_{N+1}(x)$ given by \eqref{2020A6eq1}.
For $x \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2N+3)x) \geq 0$, we may omit the summand $n=N+1$ from $f_{N+1}(x)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
& f_{N+1}(x) - f_N(x) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{N+1} - \frac{1}{N+2} \right) \sum_{n=0}^N \sin((2n+1)x) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
For $x \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2N+3)x) \leq 0$, we may insert the summand $n=N+1$ into $f_{N+1}(x)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
&f_{N+1}(x) - f_N(x) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{N+1} - \frac{1}{N+2} \right) \sum_{n=0}^{N+1} \sin((2n+1)x) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
In either case, we deduce that for $x \in (0, \pi)$, the sequence $\{f_N(x)\}_N$ is nondecreasing.

Now rewrite \eqref{2020A6eq1} as 
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq2}
f_N(x) = \sum_{n=0}^N \frac{ \sin((2n+1)x) }{2n+1}- \frac{1-\cos((2N+2)x)}{4(N+1) \sin(x)}
\end{equation}
and note that the last term tends to 0 as $N \to \infty$.
Consequently, $\lim_{N \to \infty} f_N(x)$ equals the sum of the series
\[
\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{2n+1} \sin((2n+1)x),
\]
which is the Fourier series for the ``square wave'' function defined on $(-\pi, \pi]$ by
\[
x \mapsto \begin{cases} -\frac{\pi}{4} & x \in (-\pi, 0) \\
\frac{\pi}{4} & x \in (0, \pi) \\
0 & x = 0, \pi
\end{cases}
\]
and extended periodically. Since this function is continuous on $(0, \pi)$, we deduce that the Fourier series converges to the value of the function; that is,
\[
\lim_{N \to \infty} f_N(x) = \frac{\pi}{4} \qquad (x \in (0, \pi)).
\]
This is enough to deduce the desired result as follows. 
Since
\[
f_N(x+2\pi) = f_N(x), \qquad f_N(-x) = -f_N(x),
\]
it suffices to check the bound $f_N(x) \leq \pi$ for $x \in (-\pi, \pi]$.
For $x = 0, \pi$ we have $f_N(x) = 0$ for all $N$.
For $x \in (-\pi, 0)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \geq f_0(x) \geq f_1(x) \geq \cdots
\]
For $x \in (0, \pi)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \leq f_0(x) \leq f_1(x) \leq \cdots \leq \frac{\pi}{4}
\]
and the limit is equal to $\pi/4$. We conclude that $f_N(x) \leq M$ holds for $M = \pi/4$ but not for any smaller $M$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
It is also possible to replace the use of the convergence of the Fourier series with a more direct argument; it is sufficient to do this for $x$ in a dense subset of $(0, \pi)$, such as the rational multiples of $\pi$.

Another alternative (described at \url{https://how-did-i-get-here.com/2020-putnam-a6/})
is to deduce from \eqref{2020A6eq2} and a second geometric series computation (omitted here) that
\begin{align*}
f'_N(x) &= \sum_{n=0}^N \cos((2n+1)x) - \frac{d}{dx} \left( \frac{1-\cos((2N+2)x)}{4(N+1) \sin(x)} \right) \\
&=\frac{\sin((2N+2)x)}{2\sin(x)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{(2N+2)\sin((2N+2)x) - \cos(x) (1-\cos((N+2)x)}{4(N+1)\sin(x)^2} \\
&= \frac{\cos(x) (1-\cos((N+2)x)}{4(N+1)\sin(x)^2},
\end{align*}
which is nonnegative for $x \in (0, \pi/2]$ and nonpositive for $x \in [\pi/2, \pi)$.
This implies that $f_N(x)$ always has a global maximum at $x = \pi/2$, so it suffices to check the
convergence of the Fourier series for the square wave at that point. This reduces to the Madhava--Gregory--Newton series evaluation
\[
1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

descriptive_long.question:
For a positive integer $\upperindex$, let $\finitefunc$\footnote{Corrected from $F_N$ in the source.} be the function defined by 
\[
\finitefunc(\realvar) = \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \frac{\upperindex+1/2-\indexvar}{(\upperindex+1)(2\indexvar+1)} \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar).
\]
Determine the smallest constant $M$ such that $\finitefunc(\realvar) \leq M$ for all $\upperindex$ and all real $\realvar$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The smallest constant $M$ is $\pi/4$.

We start from the expression
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq1}
\finitefunc(\realvar) = \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{2\indexvar+1} - \frac{1}{\upperindex+1} \right) \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar).
\end{equation}
Note that if $\sin(\realvar) > 0$, then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar) &= \frac{1}{2i} \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} (e^{i(2\indexvar+1)\realvar} - e^{-i(2\indexvar+1)\realvar}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2\upperindex+3)\realvar} - e^{i\realvar}}{e^{2i\realvar} - 1}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2\upperindex+3)\realvar} - e^{-i\realvar}}{e^{-2i\realvar} - 1} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2\upperindex+2)\realvar} - 1}{e^{i\realvar} - e^{-i\realvar}}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2\upperindex+2)\realvar} - 1}{e^{-i\realvar} - e^{i\realvar}} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \frac{e^{i(2\upperindex+2)\realvar}+ e^{-i(2\upperindex+2)\realvar} - 2}{e^{i\realvar} - e^{-i\realvar}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cos ((2\upperindex+2)\realvar) - 2}{2i(2i \sin(\realvar))} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos ((2\upperindex+2)\realvar)}{2\sin(\realvar)} \geq 0.
\end{align*}
We use this to compare the expressions of $\finitefunc(\realvar)$ and $\finitefunc_{\upperindex+1}(\realvar)$ given by \eqref{2020A6eq1}.
For $\realvar \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2\upperindex+3)\realvar) \geq 0$, we may omit the summand $\indexvar=\upperindex+1$ from $\finitefunc_{\upperindex+1}(\realvar)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
& \finitefunc_{\upperindex+1}(\realvar) - \finitefunc(\realvar) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{\upperindex+1} - \frac{1}{\upperindex+2} \right) \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
For $\realvar \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2\upperindex+3)\realvar) \leq 0$, we may insert the summand $\indexvar=\upperindex+1$ into $\finitefunc_{\upperindex+1}(\realvar)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
&\finitefunc_{\upperindex+1}(\realvar) - \finitefunc(\realvar) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{\upperindex+1} - \frac{1}{\upperindex+2} \right) \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex+1} \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
In either case, we deduce that for $\realvar \in (0, \pi)$, the sequence $\{\finitefunc(\realvar)\}_{\upperindex}$ is nondecreasing.

Now rewrite \eqref{2020A6eq1} as 
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq2}
\finitefunc(\realvar) = \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \frac{ \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar) }{2\indexvar+1}- \frac{1-\cos((2\upperindex+2)\realvar)}{4(\upperindex+1) \sin(\realvar)}
\end{equation}
and note that the last term tends to 0 as $\upperindex \to \infty$.
Consequently, $\lim_{\upperindex \to \infty} \finitefunc(\realvar)$ equals the sum of the series
\[
\sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\indexvar+1} \sin((2\indexvar+1)\realvar),
\]
which is the Fourier series for the ``square wave'' function defined on $(-\pi, \pi]$ by
\[
\realvar \mapsto \begin{cases} -\frac{\pi}{4} & \realvar \in (-\pi, 0) \\
\frac{\pi}{4} & \realvar \in (0, \pi) \\
0 & \realvar = 0, \pi
\end{cases}
\]
and extended periodically. Since this function is continuous on $(0, \pi)$, we deduce that the Fourier series converges to the value of the function; that is,
\[
\lim_{\upperindex \to \infty} \finitefunc(\realvar) = \frac{\pi}{4} \qquad (\realvar \in (0, \pi)).
\]
This is enough to deduce the desired result as follows. 
Since
\[
\finitefunc(\realvar+2\pi) = \finitefunc(\realvar), \qquad \finitefunc(-\realvar) = -\finitefunc(\realvar),
\]
it suffices to check the bound $\finitefunc(\realvar) \leq \pi$ for $\realvar \in (-\pi, \pi]$.
For $\realvar = 0, \pi$ we have $\finitefunc(\realvar) = 0$ for all $\upperindex$.
For $\realvar \in (-\pi, 0)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \geq \finitefunc_{0}(\realvar) \geq \finitefunc_{1}(\realvar) \geq \cdots
\]
For $\realvar \in (0, \pi)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \leq \finitefunc_{0}(\realvar) \leq \finitefunc_{1}(\realvar) \leq \cdots \leq \frac{\pi}{4}
\]
and the limit is equal to $\pi/4$. We conclude that $\finitefunc(\realvar) \leq M$ holds for $M = \pi/4$ but not for any smaller $M$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
It is also possible to replace the use of the convergence of the Fourier series with a more direct argument; it is sufficient to do this for $\realvar$ in a dense subset of $(0, \pi)$, such as the rational multiples of $\pi$.

Another alternative (described at \url{https://how-did-i-get-here.com/2020-putnam-a6/})
is to deduce from \eqref{2020A6eq2} and a second geometric series computation (omitted here) that
\begin{align*}
\finitefunc'(\realvar) &= \sum_{\indexvar=0}^{\upperindex} \cos((2\indexvar+1)\realvar) - \frac{d}{d\realvar} \left( \frac{1-\cos((2\upperindex+2)\realvar)}{4(\upperindex+1) \sin(\realvar)} \right) \\
&=\frac{\sin((2\upperindex+2)\realvar)}{2\sin(\realvar)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{(2\upperindex+2)\sin((2\upperindex+2)\realvar) - \cos(\realvar) (1-\cos((\upperindex+2)\realvar))}{4(\upperindex+1)\sin(\realvar)^2} \\
&= \frac{\cos(\realvar) (1-\cos((\upperindex+2)\realvar))}{4(\upperindex+1)\sin(\realvar)^2},
\end{align*}
which is nonnegative for $\realvar \in (0, \pi/2]$ and nonpositive for $\realvar \in [\pi/2, \pi)$.
This implies that $\finitefunc(\realvar)$ always has a global maximum at $\realvar = \pi/2$, so it suffices to check the
convergence of the Fourier series for the square wave at that point. This reduces to the Madhava-Gregory-Newton series evaluation
\[
1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For a positive integer $wanderlust$, let $cloudburst$\footnote{Corrected from $F_N$ in the source.} be the function defined by 
\[
cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) = \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \frac{wanderlust+1/2-crescendo}{(wanderlust+1)(2crescendo+1)} \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine).
\]
Determine the smallest constant $M$ such that $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) \leq M$ for all $wanderlust$ and all real $quartzine$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The smallest constant $M$ is $\pi/4$.

We start from the expression
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq1}
cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) = \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{2crescendo+1} - \frac{1}{wanderlust+1} \right) \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine).
\end{equation}
Note that if $\sin(quartzine) > 0$, then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine) &= \frac{1}{2i} \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \bigl(e^{i(2crescendo+1)quartzine} - e^{-i(2crescendo+1)quartzine}\bigr) \\
&= \frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2wanderlust+3)quartzine} - e^{i\,quartzine}}{e^{2i\,quartzine} - 1}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2wanderlust+3)quartzine} - e^{-i\,quartzine}}{e^{-2i\,quartzine} - 1} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2wanderlust+2)quartzine} - 1}{e^{i\,quartzine} - e^{-i\,quartzine}}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2wanderlust+2)quartzine} - 1}{e^{-i\,quartzine} - e^{i\,quartzine}} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \frac{e^{i(2wanderlust+2)quartzine}+ e^{-i(2wanderlust+2)quartzine} - 2}{e^{i\,quartzine} - e^{-i\,quartzine}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cos ((2wanderlust+2)quartzine) - 2}{2i(2i \sin(quartzine))} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos ((2wanderlust+2)quartzine)}{2\sin(quartzine)} \ge 0.
\end{align*}
We use this to compare the expressions of $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)$ and $cloudburst_{wanderlust+1}(quartzine)$ given by \eqref{2020A6eq1}.
For $quartzine \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2wanderlust+3)quartzine) \ge 0$, we may omit the summand $crescendo=wanderlust+1$ from $cloudburst_{wanderlust+1}(quartzine)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
& cloudburst_{wanderlust+1}(quartzine) - cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) \\
&\ge \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{wanderlust+1} - \frac{1}{wanderlust+2} \right) \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine) \ge 0.
\end{align*}
For $quartzine \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2wanderlust+3)quartzine) \le 0$, we may insert the summand $crescendo=wanderlust+1$ into $cloudburst_{wanderlust+1}(quartzine)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
&cloudburst_{wanderlust+1}(quartzine) - cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) \\
&\ge \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{wanderlust+1} - \frac{1}{wanderlust+2} \right) \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust+1} \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine) \ge 0.
\end{align*}
In either case, we deduce that for $quartzine \in (0, \pi)$, the sequence $\{cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)\}_{wanderlust}$ is nondecreasing.

Now rewrite \eqref{2020A6eq1} as 
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq2}
cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine) = \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \frac{ \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine) }{2crescendo+1}- \frac{1-\cos((2wanderlust+2)quartzine)}{4(wanderlust+1) \sin(quartzine)}
\end{equation}
and note that the last term tends to $0$ as $wanderlust \to \infty$.
Consequently,
\[
\lim_{wanderlust \to \infty} cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)= \sum_{crescendo=0}^\infty \frac{1}{2crescendo+1} \sin((2crescendo+1)quartzine),
\]
which is the Fourier series for the ``square wave'' function defined on $(-\pi, \pi]$ by
\[
quartzine \mapsto \begin{cases} -\dfrac{\pi}{4} & quartzine \in (-\pi, 0),\\[6pt]
\dfrac{\pi}{4} & quartzine \in (0, \pi),\\[6pt]
0 & quartzine = 0,\pi.
\end{cases}
\]
Since this function is continuous on $(0, \pi)$, the Fourier series converges to its value; that is,
\[
\lim_{wanderlust \to \infty} cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)=\frac{\pi}{4}\qquad (quartzine \in (0, \pi)).
\]

This is enough to deduce the desired result as follows.
Since
\[
cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine+2\pi)=cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine),\qquad
cloudburst_{wanderlust}(-quartzine)=-cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine),
\]
it suffices to check the bound $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)\le \pi$ for $quartzine\in(-\pi,\pi]$.
For $quartzine=0,\pi$ we have $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)=0$ for all $wanderlust$.
For $quartzine\in(-\pi,0)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \ge cloudburst_{0}(quartzine)\ge cloudburst_{1}(quartzine)\ge\cdots
\]
For $quartzine\in(0,\pi)$, they imply that
\[
0 \le cloudburst_{0}(quartzine)\le cloudburst_{1}(quartzine)\le\cdots\le\frac{\pi}{4},
\]
and the limit is $\pi/4$. We conclude that $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)\le M$ holds for $M=\pi/4$ but not for any smaller $M$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
It is also possible to replace the use of the convergence of the Fourier series with a more direct argument; it is sufficient to do this for $quartzine$ in a dense subset of $(0, \pi)$, such as the rational multiples of $\pi$.

Another alternative (described at \url{https://how-did-i-get-here.com/2020-putnam-a6/})
is to deduce from \eqref{2020A6eq2} and a second geometric series computation (omitted here) that
\begin{align*}
cloudburst'_{wanderlust}(quartzine) &= \sum_{crescendo=0}^{wanderlust} \cos((2crescendo+1)quartzine) - \frac{d}{dquartzine} \left( \frac{1-\cos((2wanderlust+2)quartzine)}{4(wanderlust+1) \sin(quartzine)} \right) \\
&=\frac{\sin((2wanderlust+2)quartzine)}{2\sin(quartzine)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{(2wanderlust+2)\sin((2wanderlust+2)quartzine) - \cos(quartzine) \bigl(1-\cos((wanderlust+2)quartzine)\bigr)}{4(wanderlust+1)\sin(quartzine)^2} \\
&= \frac{\cos(quartzine)\bigl(1-\cos((wanderlust+2)quartzine)\bigr)}{4(wanderlust+1)\sin(quartzine)^2},
\end{align*}
which is nonnegative for $quartzine \in (0, \pi/2]$ and nonpositive for $quartzine \in [\pi/2, \pi)$.
This implies that $cloudburst_{wanderlust}(quartzine)$ always has a global maximum at $quartzine=\pi/2$, so it suffices to check the convergence of the Fourier series for the square wave at that point. This reduces to the Madhava--Gregory--Newton series evaluation
\[
1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For a positive integer $infinite$, let $nonfunction$ be the function defined by 
\[
nonfunction(constant) = \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \frac{infinite+1/2-continuous}{(infinite+1)(2continuous+1)} \sin((2continuous+1)constant).
\]
Determine the smallest constant $M$ such that $nonfunction(constant) \leq M$ for all $infinite$ and all real $constant$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The smallest constant $M$ is $\pi/4$.

We start from the expression
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq1}
nonfunction(constant) = \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{2continuous+1} - \frac{1}{infinite+1} \right) \sin((2continuous+1)constant).
\end{equation}
Note that if $\sin(constant) > 0$, then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \sin((2continuous+1)constant) &= \frac{1}{2i} \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} (e^{i(2continuous+1)constant} - e^{-i(2continuous+1)constant}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2infinite+3)constant} - e^{i constant}}{e^{2i constant} - 1}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2infinite+3)constant} - e^{-i constant}}{e^{-2i constant} - 1} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2infinite+2)constant} - 1}{e^{i constant} - e^{-i constant}}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2infinite+2)constant} - 1}{e^{-i constant} - e^{i constant}} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \frac{e^{i(2infinite+2)constant}+ e^{-i(2infinite+2)constant} - 2}{e^{i constant} - e^{-i constant}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cos ((2infinite+2)constant) - 2}{2i(2i \sin(constant))} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos ((2infinite+2)constant)}{2\sin(constant)} \geq 0.
\end{align*}
We use this to compare the expressions of $nonfunction(constant)$ and $f_{infinite+1}(constant)$ given by \eqref{2020A6eq1}.
For $constant \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2infinite+3)constant) \geq 0$, we may omit the summand $continuous=infinite+1$ from $f_{infinite+1}(constant)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
& f_{infinite+1}(constant) - nonfunction(constant) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{infinite+1} - \frac{1}{infinite+2} \right) \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \sin((2continuous+1)constant) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
For $constant \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2infinite+3)constant) \leq 0$, we may insert the summand $continuous=infinite+1$ into $f_{infinite+1}(constant)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
&f_{infinite+1}(constant) - nonfunction(constant) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{infinite+1} - \frac{1}{infinite+2} \right) \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite+1} \sin((2continuous+1)constant) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
In either case, we deduce that for $constant \in (0, \pi)$, the sequence $\{nonfunction(constant)\}_{infinite}$ is nondecreasing.

Now rewrite \eqref{2020A6eq1} as 
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq2}
nonfunction(constant) = \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \frac{ \sin((2continuous+1)constant) }{2continuous+1}- \frac{1-\cos((2infinite+2)constant)}{4(infinite+1) \sin(constant)}
\end{equation}
and note that the last term tends to 0 as $infinite \to \infty$.
Consequently, $\lim_{infinite \to \infty} nonfunction(constant)$ equals the sum of the series
\[
\sum_{continuous=0}^\infty \frac{1}{2continuous+1} \sin((2continuous+1)constant),
\]
which is the Fourier series for the ``square wave'' function defined on $(-\pi, \pi]$ by
\[
constant \mapsto \begin{cases} -\frac{\pi}{4} & constant \in (-\pi, 0) \\
\frac{\pi}{4} & constant \in (0, \pi) \\
0 & constant = 0, \pi
\end{cases}
\]
and extended periodically. Since this function is continuous on $(0, \pi)$, we deduce that the Fourier series converges to the value of the function; that is,
\[
\lim_{infinite \to \infty} nonfunction(constant) = \frac{\pi}{4} \qquad (constant \in (0, \pi)).
\]
This is enough to deduce the desired result as follows. 
Since
\[
nonfunction(constant+2\pi) = nonfunction(constant), \qquad nonfunction(-constant) = -nonfunction(constant),
\]
it suffices to check the bound $nonfunction(constant) \leq \pi$ for $constant \in (-\pi, \pi]$.
For $constant = 0, \pi$ we have $nonfunction(constant) = 0$ for all $infinite$.
For $constant \in (-\pi, 0)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \geq f_0(constant) \geq f_1(constant) \geq \cdots
\]
For $constant \in (0, \pi)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \leq f_0(constant) \leq f_1(constant) \leq \cdots \leq \frac{\pi}{4}
\]
and the limit is equal to $\pi/4$. We conclude that $nonfunction(constant) \leq M$ holds for $M = \pi/4$ but not for any smaller $M$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
It is also possible to replace the use of the convergence of the Fourier series with a more direct argument; it is sufficient to do this for $constant$ in a dense subset of $(0, \pi)$, such as the rational multiples of $\pi$.

Another alternative (described at \url{https://how-did-i-get-here.com/2020-putnam-a6/})
is to deduce from \eqref{2020A6eq2} and a second geometric series computation (omitted here) that
\begin{align*}
f'_{infinite}(constant) &= \sum_{continuous=0}^{infinite} \cos((2continuous+1)constant) - \frac{d}{dconstant} \left( \frac{1-\cos((2infinite+2)constant)}{4(infinite+1) \sin(constant)} \right) \\
&=\frac{\sin((2infinite+2)constant)}{2\sin(constant)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{(2infinite+2)\sin((2infinite+2)constant) - \cos(constant) (1-\cos((infinite+2)constant)}{4(infinite+1)\sin(constant)^2} \\
&= \frac{\cos(constant) (1-\cos((infinite+2)constant)}{4(infinite+1)\sin(constant)^2},
\end{align*}
which is nonnegative for $constant \in (0, \pi/2]$ and nonpositive for $constant \in [\pi/2, \pi)$.
This implies that $nonfunction(constant)$ always has a global maximum at $constant = \pi/2$, so it suffices to check the
convergence of the Fourier series for the square wave at that point. This reduces to the Madhava--Gregory--Newton series evaluation
\[
1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

garbled_string.question:
Problem:
<<<
For a positive integer $zclhrmdo$, let $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}$\footnote{Corrected from $F_N$ in the source.} be the function defined by 
\[
qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) = \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \frac{zclhrmdo+1/2-kjdspvla}{(zclhrmdo+1)(2kjdspvla+1)} \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq).
\]
Determine the smallest constant $M$ such that $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) \leq M$ for all $zclhrmdo$ and all real $grmbltxq$.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
Solution:
<<<
The smallest constant $M$ is $\pi/4$.

We start from the expression
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq1}
qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) = \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2}{2kjdspvla+1} - \frac{1}{zclhrmdo+1} \right) \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq).
\end{equation}
Note that if $\sin(grmbltxq) > 0$, then
\begin{align*}
\sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq) &= \frac{1}{2i} \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} (e^{i(2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq} - e^{-i(2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2zclhrmdo+3)grmbltxq} - e^{igrmbltxq}}{e^{2igrmbltxq} - 1}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2zclhrmdo+3)grmbltxq} - e^{-igrmbltxq}}{e^{-2igrmbltxq} - 1} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \left( \frac{e^{i(2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq} - 1}{e^{igrmbltxq} - e^{-igrmbltxq}}  -
\frac{e^{-i(2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq} - 1}{e^{-igrmbltxq} - e^{igrmbltxq}} \right) \\
&=\frac{1}{2i} \frac{e^{i(2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq}+ e^{-i(2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq} - 2}{e^{igrmbltxq} - e^{-igrmbltxq}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cos ((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq) - 2}{2i(2i \sin(grmbltxq))} \\
&= \frac{1 - \cos ((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq)}{2\sin(grmbltxq)} \geq 0.
\end{align*}
We use this to compare the expressions of $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq)$ and $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo+1}(grmbltxq)$ given by \eqref{2020A6eq1}.
For $grmbltxq \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2zclhrmdo+3)grmbltxq) \geq 0$, we may omit the summand $kjdspvla=zclhrmdo+1$ from $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo+1}(grmbltxq)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
& qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo+1}(grmbltxq) - qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{zclhrmdo+1} - \frac{1}{zclhrmdo+2} \right) \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
For $grmbltxq \in (0, \pi)$ with $\sin((2zclhrmdo+3)grmbltxq) \leq 0$, we may insert the summand $kjdspvla=zclhrmdo+1$ into $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo+1}(grmbltxq)$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
&qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo+1}(grmbltxq) - qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) \\
&\geq \frac{1}{2}  \left( \frac{1}{zclhrmdo+1} - \frac{1}{zclhrmdo+2} \right) \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo+1} \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq) \geq 0.
\end{align*}
In either case, we deduce that for $grmbltxq \in (0, \pi)$, the sequence $\{qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq)\}_{zclhrmdo}$ is nondecreasing.

Now rewrite \eqref{2020A6eq1} as 
\begin{equation} \label{2020A6eq2}
qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) = \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \frac{ \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq) }{2kjdspvla+1}- \frac{1-\cos((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq)}{4(zclhrmdo+1) \sin(grmbltxq)}
\end{equation}
and note that the last term tends to 0 as $zclhrmdo \to \infty$.
Consequently, $\lim_{zclhrmdo \to \infty} qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq)$ equals the sum of the series
\[
\sum_{kjdspvla=0}^\infty \frac{1}{2kjdspvla+1} \sin((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq),
\]
which is the Fourier series for the ``square wave'' function defined on $(-\pi, \pi]$ by
\[
gr mbltxq \mapsto \begin{cases} -\frac{\pi}{4} & grmbltxq \in (-\pi, 0) \\
\frac{\pi}{4} & grmbltxq \in (0, \pi) \\
0 & grmbltxq = 0, \pi
\end{cases}
\]
and extended periodically. Since this function is continuous on $(0, \pi)$, we deduce that the Fourier series converges to the value of the function; that is,
\[
\lim_{zclhrmdo \to \infty} qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) = \frac{\pi}{4} \qquad (grmbltxq \in (0, \pi)).
\]
This is enough to deduce the desired result as follows. 
Since
\[
qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq+2\pi) = qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq), \qquad qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(-grmbltxq) = -qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq),
\]
it suffices to check the bound $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) \leq \pi$ for $grmbltxq \in (-\pi, \pi]$.
For $grmbltxq = 0, \pi$ we have $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) = 0$ for all $zclhrmdo$.
For $grmbltxq \in (-\pi, 0)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \geq qzxwvtnp_{0}(grmbltxq) \geq qzxwvtnp_{1}(grmbltxq) \geq \cdots
\]
For $grmbltxq \in (0, \pi)$, the previous arguments imply that
\[
0 \leq qzxwvtnp_{0}(grmbltxq) \leq qzxwvtnp_{1}(grmbltxq) \leq \cdots \leq \frac{\pi}{4}
\]
and the limit is equal to $\pi/4$. We conclude that $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) \leq M$ holds for $M = \pi/4$ but not for any smaller $M$, as desired.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
It is also possible to replace the use of the convergence of the Fourier series with a more direct argument; it is sufficient to do this for $grmbltxq$ in a dense subset of $(0, \pi)$, such as the rational multiples of $\pi$.

Another alternative (described at \url{https://how-did-i-get-here.com/2020-putnam-a6/})
is to deduce from \eqref{2020A6eq2} and a second geometric series computation (omitted here) that
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp'_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq) &= \sum_{kjdspvla=0}^{zclhrmdo} \cos((2kjdspvla+1)grmbltxq) - \frac{d}{dgrmbltxq} \left( \frac{1-\cos((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq)}{4(zclhrmdo+1) \sin(grmbltxq)} \right) \\
&=\frac{\sin((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq)}{2\sin(grmbltxq)} \\
&\qquad - \frac{(2zclhrmdo+2)\sin((2zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq) - \cos(grmbltxq) (1-\cos((zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq))}{4(zclhrmdo+1)\sin(grmbltxq)^2} \\
&= \frac{\cos(grmbltxq) (1-\cos((zclhrmdo+2)grmbltxq))}{4(zclhrmdo+1)\sin(grmbltxq)^2},
\end{align*}
which is nonnegative for $grmbltxq \in (0, \pi/2]$ and nonpositive for $grmbltxq \in [\pi/2, \pi)$.
This implies that $qzxwvtnp_{zclhrmdo}(grmbltxq)$ always has a global maximum at $grmbltxq = \pi/2$, so it suffices to check the
convergence of the Fourier series for the square wave at that point. This reduces to the Madhava--Gregory--Newton series evaluation
\[
1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
For every non-negative integer N define the 2\pi -periodic, odd trigonometric polynomial

g_N(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{N}\frac{N+\dfrac32-n}{(N+2)(2n+1)}\,\sin\bigl((2n+1)x\bigr),\qquad x\in\mathbb R.

Determine the smallest real number M for which the inequality

g_N(x)\le M\qquad(\forall N\ge 0,\;\forall x\in\mathbb R)

holds simultaneously for every choice of N and x.
kernel_variant.solution:
Answer.
M = \dfrac{\pi}{4}.

Throughout we put

S_m(x):=\sum_{k=0}^{m}\frac{\sin((2k+1)x)}{2k+1}\qquad(m\ge 0),

so that S_m is the (2m+1)-st Dirichlet partial sum of the Fourier series of the 2\pi -periodic square-wave

F(x):=\tfrac{\pi}{4}\,\operatorname{sgn}(\sin x)\quad(-\pi<x<\pi).

1.  Abel-summation identity.

Write
c_n:=\dfrac{N+\tfrac32-n}{N+2}\;(0\le n\le N),\qquad d_n(x):=\dfrac{\sin((2n+1)x)}{2n+1}.
Since c_n-c_{n+1}=\dfrac1{N+2}, Abel summation gives

\begin{aligned}
g_N(x)
 &=\sum_{n=0}^{N}c_n d_n(x)
   =c_{N}S_{N}(x)+\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(c_{n}-c_{n+1})S_{n}(x)\\[4pt]
 &=\frac{\tfrac32}{N+2}S_{N}(x)+\frac1{N+2}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}S_{n}(x)\\[4pt]
 &=\frac{N+1}{N+2}\,\sigma_{N}(x)+\frac{1}{2(N+2)}S_{N}(x),\tag{1}
\end{aligned}
where

\sigma_{N}(x):=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_{n}(x)\qquad(N\ge0)

is the N-th Cesaro (Fejer) mean of the sine series of F.

2.  A uniform bound for the Dirichlet sums.

Lemma 2.1   For every integer m\geq 0 and every real number x,
|S_m(x)|\le \tfrac{\pi}{2}.

(The standard proof uses the identity
S_m(x)=\displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}\frac{\sin\bigl(2(m+1)t\bigr)}{2\sin t}\,dt.)

3.  A uniform bound for the Cesaro means \sigma _N.

The Fejer kernel
K_{N}(t):=\frac{1}{N+1}\Bigl(\frac{\sin\frac{N+1}{2}t}{\sin\frac{t}{2}}\Bigr)^2 \ge 0
satisfies (1/2\pi )\int _{-\pi }^{\pi }K_{N}(t)dt = 1.  A standard computation shows

\sigma _N(x)=\frac1{2\pi }\int_{-\pi }^{\pi } F(x-t)K_{N}(t)\,dt.\tag{2}

Because K_{N}\ge0 and |F|\le\pi/4 everywhere, (2) implies

|\sigma _N(x)|\le\frac{\pi}{4}\qquad(\forall N\ge0,\;\forall x).\tag{3}

4.  The global upper bound for g_N.

Insert the bounds (3) and Lemma 2.1 into identity (1):

\begin{aligned}
|g_N(x)|&\le \frac{N+1}{N+2}\,|\sigma _N(x)|+\frac{1}{2(N+2)}|S_{N}(x)|\\[4pt]
&\le \frac{N+1}{N+2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2(N+2)}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2}\\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{4}\Bigl(\frac{N+1}{N+2}+\frac{1}{N+2}\Bigr)=\frac{\pi}{4}.\tag{4}
\end{aligned}

Since g_N is odd, inequality (4) yields in particular

g_N(x)\le \frac{\pi}{4}\qquad(\forall N,\,\forall x).\tag{5}

5.  Sharpness of \pi /4.

Take x = \pi /2.  Because \sin((2n+1)\pi /2)=(-1)^n,

\begin{aligned}
g_N(\tfrac{\pi }{2})&=\sum_{n=0}^{N}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}-\frac{1-(-1)^{N+1}}{4(N+2)}.\tag{6}
\end{aligned}

When N = 2m+1 is odd, the second term in (6) vanishes, and letting m\to \infty  gives

\lim_{m\to\infty} g_{2m+1}(\tfrac{\pi }{2}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} = \arctan(1)=\frac{\pi}{4}.\tag{7}

Thus values of g_N(x) come arbitrarily close to \pi /4 from below, so no smaller constant can work in (5).

Combining (5) with (7) we conclude that the smallest admissible constant is

M = \boxed{\dfrac{\pi}{4}}.

core_steps: Algebraically split each coefficient so that (N+½−n)/((N+1)(2n+1)) = ½(2/(2n+1) − 1/(N+1))
Compute the finite sum Σ_{n=0}^N sin((2n+1)x) via a geometric-series trick and use it to prove f_{N+1}(x) − f_N(x) ≥ 0 on (0,π)
Conclude that, for each fixed x, {f_N(x)}_N is monotone; hence its supremum equals its limit
Rewrite f_N(x)=Σ_{n=0}^N sin((2n+1)x)/(2n+1) − vanishing error, so the limit is the odd-harmonic Fourier series of the square wave
Evaluate that series (Gregory–Leibniz/Madhava) to get π/4 and extend the bound to all x by periodicity and oddness

slot1.description: The constant added to N in the denominator (N+1) can be replaced by any fixed positive real c

slot2.description: The constant added to N in the numerator must track slot1 as (N+c−½) so that the algebraic split in step 1 still works
